name: Abu Saeed al-Khudri
title: Abu Saeed al-Khudri (RA): The Scholar of Madinah
meta_description: The story of Abu Saeed al-Khudri (RA), a boy of Madinah who lost his father at Uhud, chose patience over asking, and grew into one of the great teachers of the Prophet Muhammad's words.
keywords: Abu Saeed al-Khudri, story of Abu Saeed, Mufti of Madinah, hadith narrators, sahaba stories, companions of the Prophet, Uhud
lede: You have almost certainly heard his words without ever hearing his name. The hadith that what lies between the Prophet's home and his pulpit is a garden of Paradise, the hadith of the man who killed ninety-nine and was still forgiven: these reach us through one quiet man.

This is the story of Abu Saeed al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him): a boy of Madinah who lost his father young, learned to ask Allah before asking people, and spent a long life carefully passing on what he had seen and heard.

paras: His name was Sa'd ibn Malik, though he is known to us as Abu Saeed al-Khudri, a name that points back to a famous ancestor of his people. He was from the tribe of Khazraj, one of the two great tribes of the Ansar, the helpers of Madinah.

He was ten years old when the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ arrived in the city. His mother and his father had both accepted Islam, and they brought their young son to give his pledge. His mother was among the women who pledged directly to the Prophet ﷺ herself, not through her husband. Picture a boy of ten standing between his parents, learning from them what it meant to belong to this.

His was a family woven into this faith. His mother had been married before, and her children from that marriage also became companions, among them his half-brother Qatadah ibn al-Nu'man. His own sister, al-Furay'ah bint Malik, would later be at the centre of a ruling of the religion when her husband died and the question of a widow's waiting period was settled in her case.
He was Madani through and through. He was in the city before the Prophet ﷺ reached it, he lived his whole life within it, and he would one day be buried in its earth. He almost never left.

sections.h2: The day his father did not come home
paras: When the Battle of Uhud came, Abu Saeed was thirteen. His father took him by the hand and presented him to the Prophet ﷺ as a fighter. The Prophet ﷺ looked the boy up and down and turned him back: he was not yet old enough. So his father walked him home, and that was the last memory he kept of him.

He waited with the other children, away from the battlefield, while their fathers and uncles fought. His father, Malik ibn Sinan, was among those who did not flee. When the Prophet ﷺ was struck and bleeding, Malik caught some of that blood, and rather than let it fall to the ground or wipe it away, he put it to his own lips. Moments later he was killed.

The Prophet ﷺ said of him, for anyone who wished to look at a man whose blood had mixed with his own, look at Malik ibn Sinan. Then he rode out to the waiting children, found the boy, and gently told him himself: your father has been martyred, may Allah magnify your reward.

sections.h2: The words that changed his hand
paras: Grief was not the only weight. The family sank into poverty, and Abu Saeed was now the one responsible for his widowed mother. She sent him to ask the Prophet ﷺ for help.

He approached, but before he could speak he heard the Prophet ﷺ addressing the Ansar. Whatever good comes to me, he was saying, I will never withhold it from you. And then: whoever seeks dignity, Allah will give him dignity; whoever seeks to be self-sufficient, Allah will make him self-sufficient; and no one is given a gift better or wider than patience.

The boy stopped. He had come to ask, and instead he decided he would not. He would seek it from Allah alone. He went home, told his mother, and they turned to Allah. Blessing came down on their household in quiet, ordinary ways, in the produce of his garden above all, and he later said he did not know a single household of the Ansar that became wealthier than theirs.

Something of that lesson stayed in how he listened. Once the Prophet ﷺ told him directly that whoever is content with Allah as his Lord, with Islam as his religion, and with Muhammad ﷺ as his Prophet, Paradise becomes his. Abu Saeed asked him to say it again, wanting to be sure he had heard it right, and carried it for the rest of his life.

Abu Saeed grew up beside the Prophet ﷺ, and he watched closely. He fought in every battle after Uhud, and he was among the people who gave the pledge under the tree, the pledge Allah Himself was pleased with.

He rarely spoke about himself, so we know him mostly through what he carried of the Prophet ﷺ. He described a man more shy than a young bride in her room, whose displeasure you could read in his face. He saw the Prophet ﷺ as a guest in his small home, praying in a single worn garment, and he saw him as a host, visiting Abu Saeed himself when he was ill.

He never forgot one rainy night in the mosque, when the roof of palm leaves leaked and he looked up to see mud and water on the forehead and nose of the Prophet ﷺ as he prostrated. He remembered, too, the day the Prophet ﷺ came upon a group of believers so poor their clothing barely covered them, sitting together while one read Quran and made supplication. The Prophet ﷺ sat with them and praised Allah for placing such people in his community, and gave them the glad tidings that the weak and the poor would enter Paradise long before the rest.
From this one man we learn how the Prophet ﷺ ate, how he dressed, how he travelled, how he prayed. He carried it all carefully, and then he gave it away.

sections.h2: Mercy, even with a sword at the door
paras: He lived a long time, long enough to see hard years come to Madinah. In one such year the city was attacked, and some of those attacking it still called themselves Muslims. Abu Saeed, who knew Madinah better than anyone, was chased into a cave.

A man entered after him, sword in hand. Abu Saeed had a weapon of his own, but he would not use it. He recited the words of the son of Adam to his brother, who refused to return violence with violence:

The man, hearing this, asked who he was. When he learned he was a companion of the Prophet ﷺ, his hand fell. He asked Abu Saeed to pray that Allah forgive him, and then he left him in peace. Abu Saeed had not only narrated mercy; he lived it with a blade at his chest.

ayah.text: If you raise your hand to kill me, I will not raise mine to kill you. I fear God, the Lord of all worlds, and I would rather you were burdened with my sins as well as yours and became an inhabitant of the Fire: such is the evildoers' reward.

paras: When the Prophet ﷺ passed away, Abu Saeed was twenty-three. He became one of the men people came to for knowledge, until they were travelling from distant lands to sit at his feet. He would teach the Quran five verses at a time, giving each its context and its meaning, then returning later to teach five more.

He had a particular welcome for students of knowledge. He would spread out his cloak for them and say that the Prophet ﷺ had instructed the believers to take care of those who came seeking the religion: welcome, he would tell them, to those we were told to care for. Spread the words of the Prophet ﷺ, he urged, for that is what keeps them alive.

He was not silent before the powerful. He had heard the Prophet ﷺ say that no one should let fear of people stop him from speaking the truth, and he tried to live by it, even advising rulers who were doing wrong. He wept that he had not spoken more forcefully still. Yet through the divisions of his time he kept praying with everyone and held the community together, careful never to tear it apart.

Abu Saeed had narrated much from the Prophet ﷺ about death: how to gently remind the dying to say there is no god but Allah, how the righteous soul, carried to its grave, pleads to be taken forward quickly. As he himself lay dying in his eighties, his sons sat with him and reminded him of those very words, the words he had taught them.

He took one of his sons by the hand and walked to a particular spot in the Baqi, the burial ground of Madinah, near the grave of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, the man for whom the Throne of Allah shook. Bury me right here, he said, and he chose even the cloth he would be wrapped in. He kept praying to the very end, using only the gestures of his body when he could no longer stand.

When the scholar of Madinah died, the people came from far away to pray over him: the companions still living, and the students who had learned at his side. He left behind two sons who carried his narrations onward, and a body of the Prophet's ﷺ words that we still repeat today.

lessons_heading: What Abu Saeed's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His was not a life of one famous moment. It was decades of quiet faithfulness, and that is exactly what made him a vessel for so much good.

lessons.bold: Ask Allah before you ask people.
lessons.text: A grieving boy went to ask for help and chose patience instead. He sought from Allah, and his household was never poorer for it.

lessons.bold: Pay close attention to good people.
lessons.text: Abu Saeed watched the Prophet ﷺ carefully, in public and in private, and so we know him through one boy's careful eyes. What we notice in others, we can pass on.

lessons.bold: Mercy is strongest when it costs you.
lessons.text: With a sword at his chest he refused to return harm with harm. He had narrated mercy for years; here he simply lived it.
He sought no fame and rarely spoke of himself, yet his words still shape how millions pray and remember. Sincerity carries further than noise.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah, known as the Scholar or Mufti of Madinah. His given name was Sa'd ibn Malik. He is counted among the seven companions who narrated the most hadith, with over a thousand narrations attributed to him.

faq.q: Why did Abu Saeed not fight at the Battle of Uhud?
faq.a: He was only thirteen, and when his father presented him to the Prophet ﷺ as a fighter, the Prophet ﷺ judged him too young and sent him home. His father, Malik ibn Sinan, fought and was martyred in that battle.

faq.q: Why is Abu Saeed important for hadith?
faq.a: He narrated many of the well known teachings of the Prophet ﷺ, including hadith about prayer, fasting, travel, and the virtues of good deeds. He taught for decades in Madinah, and his two sons continued passing on his narrations after him.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Abu Saeed?
faq.a: To turn to Allah before turning to people, to pay close attention to the good we witness, to hold to mercy even when it is hard, and to be faithful quietly, without seeking to be seen.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be famous, and Abu Saeed never tried to be. He lost his father as a boy, carried his family, chose patience when he could have asked for help, and then spent a long life simply remembering well and passing it on. None of it was loud. All of it lasted.

There is an invitation in his life for an ordinary day. Watch the good people around you closely. Turn to Allah before you turn to anyone else. Keep mercy in your hands even when you have been wronged. And give away what good you carry, without needing your name attached to it. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Saeed al-Khudri (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 5:28-29). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
You have heard his words a hundred times.

You may never have heard his name.

This is Abu Saeed al-Khudri (RA).

He was ten when the Prophet ﷺ reached Madinah.

Thirteen at the Battle of Uhud, judged too young to fight, and sent home by the hand.

That was the last time he walked with his father.

His father did not flee at Uhud.

He caught the Prophet's ﷺ blood so it would not touch the ground, and was martyred.

"Look at a man whose blood mixed with mine."

Then came poverty. His mother sent him to ask the Prophet ﷺ for help.

But first he heard him say: "No gift is better or wider than patience."

So the boy decided not to ask.

He sought from Allah instead.

Later he said he knew of no household of the Ansar that became wealthier than theirs.

Years on, in a hard time for Madinah, a man cornered him in a cave, sword drawn.

Abu Saeed had a blade too. He would not use it.

The man asked for prayer, and left him in peace.

He became the teacher people travelled to.

The garden of Paradise hadith. The man forgiven after ninety-nine. So much of what we know reaches us through him.

**Quiet faithfulness outlives us.**

He sought no fame, rarely spoke of himself, yet his words still shape how we pray and remember.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Abu Saeed al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him) is one of the most quoted companions of the Prophet ﷺ, and one of the least known. So many hadith we repeat without thinking reach us through this one quiet man.

He lost his father at Uhud as a boy. When poverty came and he was sent to ask the Prophet ﷺ for help, he heard him teach that no gift is better than patience, and decided instead to ask only Allah. He grew into the scholar of Madinah, and even with a sword at his chest he refused to return harm with harm.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/abu-saeed-al-khudri

email.subject: You have heard his words. You may not know his name.
email.alt_subject: The boy who chose not to ask
email.preview: Abu Saeed al-Khudri lost his father young, chose patience over asking, and carried the Prophet's words for a lifetime.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a companion whose words you have almost certainly heard, even if his name is new to you. So many of the hadith we repeat reach us through one quiet man: Abu Saeed al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him).

He was a boy of Madinah, ten years old when the Prophet ﷺ arrived. At the Battle of Uhud he was thirteen, and his father brought him forward as a fighter. The Prophet ﷺ judged him too young and sent him home. That walk home was the last time he saw his father, who was martyred that day protecting the Prophet ﷺ.

Grief was not the only weight. The family fell into poverty, and his mother sent him to ask the Prophet ﷺ for help. But before he could speak, he heard the Prophet ﷺ telling the people: whoever seeks dignity, Allah will give him dignity; whoever seeks to be self-sufficient, Allah will make him so; and no gift is better or wider than patience.

The boy stopped. He had come to ask, and he decided he would not. He would seek it from Allah alone. He went home, and he later said he knew of no household of the Ansar that became wealthier than theirs.

He grew into the scholar of Madinah, teaching the Quran five verses at a time and welcoming students who travelled from far away. He watched the Prophet ﷺ closely, in his home and on the road, and so we know countless small details through this one set of careful eyes.

He lived a long life, long enough to see hard years come to his city. Once a man cornered him in a cave with a drawn sword, and though Abu Saeed had a weapon of his own, he refused to raise it. The man asked him to pray for his forgiveness, and left him in peace. He had narrated mercy for decades; here he simply lived it.

There is a quiet lesson in him that is easy to miss: turn to Allah before you turn to people, and give away what good you carry without needing your name on it. He sought no fame, yet his words still shape how we pray and remember today.

May Allah be pleased with him, and let us learn from his patience and his care.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: You have probably heard his words without ever hearing his name. Many of the sayings of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ that we repeat reach us through one quiet man: Abu Saeed al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him).

He was a boy of Madinah, ten years old when the Prophet ﷺ arrived in the city. His mother and father had both accepted Islam, and they brought him to give his pledge. He grew up inside the city and almost never left it.

At the Battle of Uhud he was thirteen. His father took him by the hand and offered him as a fighter, but the Prophet ﷺ judged him too young and sent him home. That was the last memory he kept of his father, who was martyred that day, his blood mixing with the blood of the Prophet ﷺ.

Then poverty came, and his mother sent him to ask the Prophet ﷺ for help. Before he could speak, he heard the Prophet ﷺ say that no gift is better or wider than patience. So the boy decided not to ask. He sought from Allah instead, and he later said he knew of no household of the Ansar that became wealthier than theirs.

He watched the Prophet ﷺ closely, in his small home and on his journeys, and so we know him through this one boy's careful eyes: more shy than a bride in her room, praying in a worn garment, his face pressed to the wet earth on a rainy night. Abu Saeed carried it all, and then he gave it away.

He grew into the scholar of Madinah. People travelled from distant lands to learn from him, and he would spread his cloak and welcome them as those the Prophet ﷺ had told the believers to care for. Even years later, with a sword at his chest in a cave, he refused to return harm with harm.

When he lay dying in his eighties, his sons reminded him of the very words about death that he had taught them. He chose his own grave in the Baqi, near the man for whom the Throne of Allah shook, and kept praying with the gestures of his body until the end.
storybook.pullquote: No one is given a gift better or wider than patience.
storybook.reflection: Abu Saeed never sought to be known, yet his words still shape how millions pray and remember. He turned to Allah before he turned to people, watched the good around him with care, and gave away what he carried without needing his name attached. Quiet faithfulness, the kind that asks for nothing, can outlast the loudest life.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
